content.text: The Five AWS Support Plans

AWS offers five support plans, ranging from the free Basic plan to the comprehensive Enterprise plan. You choose a plan during account sign-up, but it can be changed at any time. The plan choice determines which support channels are available, how quickly AWS responds to critical issues, and what proactive advisory services you can access.

Choosing the right plan is not just a cost decision — it is a risk decision. A plan that is too limited for a production workload means paying for an outage in downtime rather than in support fees. A plan that is too expensive for a learning account wastes money that could run more experiments.
Plan Comparison

attrs.alt: AWS Support Plans comparison table: Basic, Developer, Business, Enterprise On-Ramp, Enterprise — showing pricing, response times, Trusted Advisor, Health API, TAM, and contact limits

content.text: The comparison above maps all five plans across the features that matter most for day-to-day operations. The critical differentiators are response time SLAs for production outages, access to all Trusted Advisor checks (Business+), Health API access for programmatic alerting, and the TAM relationship (Enterprise tiers). The recommendation strip at the bottom guides which plan fits which workload type.
Plan 1: Basic (Free)

Every AWS account includes Basic support at no cost. It is not a degraded version of paid support — it is a different category of service that focuses on self-service resources rather than human interaction:
AWS documentation, whitepapers, and re:Post community forums for technical questions
AWS Personal Health Dashboard — a read-only view of events affecting your account (not the programmatic API, which requires Business+)
Trusted Advisor — 7 core security and service limit checks (out of 500+)
Billing and account support via the console — all plans can open billing cases
Service quota increase requests via the console

Basic support is appropriate for personal projects, learning environments, and accounts that have no production traffic. It is not appropriate for any workload where downtime has a real cost — business, reputational, or contractual.
Plan 2: Developer ($29/month or 3% of monthly charges)

Developer support adds email access to AWS technical support engineers during business hours (09:00 to 18:00 local time of the AWS office handling your case). It is designed for teams actively building on AWS who occasionally need expert guidance but can tolerate multi-hour response times.
Key Developer Plan Details
Feature
Value
Minimum monthly cost
$29 or 3% of monthly AWS charges — whichever is greater
Technical support
Email only, business hours (typically M-F 09:00-18:00)
Number of contacts
1 designated contact can open technical cases
General guidance response
Business hours, < 24 hours
System impaired response
Business hours, < 12 hours
Case severity
General guidance and system impaired only (no production-down SLA)
Trusted Advisor
7 core checks (same as Basic)

The 3% pricing model means Developer support is effectively free for very small bills. A $200/month AWS bill results in a $6 support charge — still billed at the $29 minimum. At $967/month of AWS usage, the percentage model ($29) equals the minimum. Above that, you pay 3%.

Developer is a reasonable stepping stone if you are a solo developer or small team doing non-production work on AWS and want occasional email access to engineers. It is not suitable for production systems — the response time SLAs are measured in half-days, and there is no phone support.
Plan 3: Business ($100/month minimum)

Business support is the first plan that AWS considers appropriate for production workloads. It unlocks 24/7 access to AWS Support engineers via phone, email, and chat, plus all Trusted Advisor checks and the Health API for programmatic monitoring.
Business Plan Pricing
Monthly AWS Charges
Support Cost Calculation
Example
Minimum $100/month
10% of monthly charges
$500 on a $5,000 bill
7% of monthly charges
5% of monthly charges
3% of monthly charges

For most small-to-medium production workloads, Business support costs between $100 and $500/month — a fraction of the cost of a single hour of engineer time during a production incident. The economics strongly favor Business support for any account generating revenue or serving real customers.
What Business Support Unlocks
24/7 phone, email, and chat access to AWS Support Engineers — no waiting for business hours
All 500+ Trusted Advisor checks across five categories: cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits
AWS Health API — programmatically query AWS events and maintenance notifications, enabling automated alerting and runbook triggers
Third-party software support — guidance on AWS configurations for software like Linux OS, databases, web servers
Unlimited contacts — anyone on your team can open support cases
Contextual guidance — engineers can review specific architectures and configurations, not just documentation

# Access the AWS Health API (requires Business+ support plan)
aws health describe-events \
  --filter '{"eventStatusCodes":["open","upcoming"]}' \
  --query 'events[].{Type:eventTypeCode, Status:statusCode, Region:region, Service:service}' \
  --output table

# Get details on an event that affects your resources
aws health describe-event-details \
  --event-arns arn:aws:health:us-east-1::event/EC2/AWS_EC2_INSTANCE_STOP_SCHEDULED/AWS_EC2_INSTANCE_STOP_SCHEDULED_12345 \
  --query 'successfulSet[].eventDetails.eventDescription.latestDescription'

# Get Trusted Advisor check results (all categories with Business+)
aws support describe-trusted-advisor-checks --language en \
  --query 'checks[].{Id:id, Name:name, Category:category}' \
  --output table
Plan 4: Enterprise On-Ramp ($5,500/month minimum)

Enterprise On-Ramp bridges the gap between Business support and the full Enterprise plan. It adds a critical response time SLA (30 minutes for business-critical system down events) and access to a pool of Technical Account Managers — rotating advisors rather than a dedicated individual.
Feature
Business
Enterprise On-Ramp
Critical response (business-critical down)
Not available
< 30 minutes
Technical Account Manager
Not included
Pool of TAMs (rotating)
Concierge Support Team
Not included
Billing and account guidance
Infrastructure Event Management
Not included
Once per year
Operations Reviews
Not included
Periodic review available

The pool-of-TAMs model means you do not have a single named TAM to build a relationship with, but you can still request TAM-level guidance. This makes Enterprise On-Ramp suitable for growing companies that need TAM services occasionally but cannot justify $15,000/month for the full Enterprise plan.
Plan 5: Enterprise ($15,000/month minimum)

Enterprise support is designed for organizations running mission-critical workloads where even a 30-minute response to a critical incident is unacceptably slow. The key differentiator from Enterprise On-Ramp is a dedicated, named Technical Account Manager (TAM) and a 15-minute response for business-critical events.
Dedicated TAM: a named AWS engineer who learns your architecture, attends regular business reviews, and is your primary AWS contact
15-minute critical response: for situations where a critical system is down and every minute of downtime has measurable business impact
Infrastructure Event Management (IEM): AWS engineers actively monitor and support planned high-traffic events like product launches
Well-Architected Reviews: TAM-led reviews of your architecture against AWS best practices
Operations Reviews: monthly reviews of cost, performance, security, and reliability

Enterprise support is priced so that for large AWS spends (over $500,000/month), the percentage-based pricing converges to around 3% — similar to Business support at scale. For large enterprises, the cost per dollar of AWS spend is actually lower with Enterprise support while getting dramatically better service.
Changing Your Support Plan

You can upgrade or downgrade your support plan at any time through the AWS Support Center or the Billing and Cost Management console. The change takes effect immediately for upgrades; downgrades take effect at the start of the next billing cycle.
Log in as the root user (support plan changes are root-only)
Navigate to Support -> Support Center -> Support settings (top right)
Or go to Billing and Cost Management -> Support -> Change your support plan
Select the desired plan and confirm the change
The new plan pricing appears on your next invoice

Upgrading mid-month is prorated — you pay only for the days at the new plan level. Downgrading mid-month is also prorated; your current plan's features remain active until the end of the current billing period.

# Check your current support plan level (requires support:DescribeSupportLevel permission)
aws support describe-support-level

# Expected output format:
# {
#   "supportLevel": "business"
# }
# Possible values: basic | developer | business | enterprise-on-ramp | enterprise

# Note: changing support plan has no CLI equivalent — use the console
# This is a billing/account action, similar to updating payment methods
Trusted Advisor: Why It Matters for Plan Selection

Trusted Advisor runs automated checks against your AWS account and flags issues across five categories. The depth of Trusted Advisor access is a major practical differentiator between Basic/Developer and Business+ plans:
Category
Core Checks (Basic/Dev)
All Checks (Business+)
Security
4 checks (S3 permissions, security groups, root MFA, IAM use)
~50 checks including CloudTrail, password policies, key rotation, network ACLs
Cost Optimization
0 checks
~30 checks (idle instances, unassociated EIPs, unused EBS volumes, reserved instance recommendations)
Performance
0 checks
~10 checks (EC2 instance type recommendations, CloudFront config, EBS throughput)
Fault Tolerance
1 check (S3 bucket versioning)
~20 checks (EBS snapshots, RDS Multi-AZ, Route 53 failover, Auto Scaling groups)
Service Limits
2 checks
~50 checks covering service quotas across all major AWS services

For production accounts, the cost optimization checks alone often identify savings that exceed the Business plan's monthly cost. A single idle EC2 instance flagged by Trusted Advisor can save more per month than Business support costs.

# With Business+ plan: run all Trusted Advisor checks and get a summary
aws support describe-trusted-advisor-check-summaries \
  --check-ids $(aws support describe-trusted-advisor-checks --language en \
    --query 'checks[].id' --output text) \
  --query 'summaries[?status!=`ok`].{CheckId:checkId, Status:status}' \
  --output table

# Refresh a specific Trusted Advisor check (cost optimization: idle EC2 instances)
aws support refresh-trusted-advisor-check --check-id "Ti39halfu8"

# Get cost optimization check results
aws support describe-trusted-advisor-check-result \
  --check-id "Ti39halfu8" \
  --language "en" \
  --query 'result.flaggedResources[].{Region:region, Instance:metadata[1], Savings:metadata[6]}'
Support Plan Decision Framework
Use these questions to pick the right plan:
Question
If Yes
If No
Is this account running production traffic?
Start at Business; consider Enterprise for critical workloads
Developer or Basic may be sufficient
Does downtime have a financial or reputational cost?
Business minimum; evaluate Enterprise for < 1h recovery requirement
Developer (or Basic for pure experiments)
Do you need 24/7 phone access to AWS engineers?
Business minimum
Developer covers business-hours email access
Do you need a TAM for architectural guidance?
Enterprise On-Ramp or Enterprise
Business covers on-demand technical support
Is business-critical response time under 30 minutes required?
Enterprise On-Ramp or Enterprise
Business (1-hour production-down SLA) may suffice
Do you have compliance audits requiring support SLA documentation?
Business or higher — SLA is documented in AWS Service Terms
Basic and Developer have no SLA commitments

A common mistake is starting with Basic support for a workload that eventually becomes production, then scrambling to upgrade during an incident. Plan for the workload's future state, not its current state. Upgrading from Basic to Business during a production outage while waiting for email support is a stressful and expensive lesson.
Opening Support Cases

Regardless of plan, all technical support cases are opened through the AWS Support Center in the console. The interface prompts you to categorize the case by service, issue type, and severity. Severity selection directly affects your response time SLA:
Severity
Description
Response Time (Business plan)
General guidance
Question or non-blocking issue
< 24 hours (business hours for Developer)
System impaired
Non-critical function impaired
< 12 hours
Production system impaired
Production degraded but not down
< 4 hours
Production system down
Critical production issue, full impact
< 1 hour
Business-critical system down
Revenue-impacting, no workaround
< 15-30 min (Enterprise tiers only)

Choosing the correct severity matters. Overstating severity (opening production-down cases for general questions) uses your support capacity unfairly and may result in AWS Support asking you to reopen the case at a lower severity. Understating severity (opening general guidance for a production outage) means you wait longer than necessary.
The Technical Account Manager (TAM) Role

The Technical Account Manager is a named AWS employee who acts as your primary point of contact with AWS and provides proactive technical guidance. Understanding what a TAM does — and does not do — helps you evaluate whether Enterprise support is worth the cost for your organization.
What a TAM Does
Conducts regular operational business reviews (OBRs) — typically monthly or quarterly — where they analyze your account's cost, performance, reliability, and security trends
Provides architectural guidance for new projects and helps design solutions that align with AWS best practices and the Well-Architected Framework
Acts as an escalation path when support cases are not progressing — the TAM can engage the right engineering teams internally
Gives advance notice of upcoming service changes, deprecations, and maintenance events that may affect your workloads
Coordinates Infrastructure Event Management (IEM) for planned high-traffic events like product launches, Black Friday, or major marketing campaigns
Helps you build a cloud strategy roadmap and connects you with AWS service teams, solution architects, and training resources
What a TAM Does Not Do
A TAM is not on-call support — they do not handle incidents directly; that remains the Support Engineering team
A TAM does not write or deploy code; they advise on architecture and configuration, not implementation
A TAM does not provide legal or compliance advice — they can guide you to relevant compliance programs but cannot serve as a compliance officer
A TAM cannot override AWS pricing or service limits unilaterally — they can facilitate quota increase requests and commercial discussions but are not account executives

For organizations spending significant amounts on AWS, the TAM relationship often pays for itself through cost optimization recommendations alone. A TAM who identifies over-provisioned EC2 instances or unused Reserved Instances can save more per month than the Enterprise plan costs.
Infrastructure Event Management (IEM)

Infrastructure Event Management is a structured AWS service included with Enterprise plans that provides dedicated support during planned events. AWS assigns a team of engineers to actively monitor your systems during the event, provide real-time guidance, and have escalation paths pre-established before the event begins.
IEM is designed for events like:
Major product launches where traffic may spike dramatically from a known baseline
Large-scale migrations or database cutover windows where rollback procedures must be ready
High-traffic commercial events (Black Friday, Cyber Monday, Singles Day) for e-commerce workloads
Live streaming events or broadcasts where viewer counts could spike unpredictably

Enterprise On-Ramp includes one IEM event per year. Enterprise plans can request IEM for any planned event. Business support customers can purchase IEM as an add-on, though it is not included in the plan.

If you are running an e-commerce site or event-driven service and have a history of peak-traffic events, even the IEM benefit alone can justify Enterprise On-Ramp. Having AWS engineers watching your systems during a product launch is qualitatively different from having access to on-demand support — they catch issues before they escalate.
AWS Support in Multi-Account Organizations

When accounts belong to an AWS Organization, support plans are per-account — each account requires its own support plan. A Business plan on the management account does not extend support to member accounts; each member account needs its own paid plan to access Business-level support.
This creates a common architectural consideration: organizations often have dozens or hundreds of member accounts, and buying Business support for each adds up quickly. Common approaches:
Production accounts: Business or Enterprise — these are the accounts that need fast response times
Development and test accounts: Developer or Basic — slower response times are tolerable for non-production workloads
Security/logging accounts: Business — the security account often runs GuardDuty, Security Hub, and CloudTrail aggregation; you want fast support if these break
Sandbox accounts: Basic — exploratory accounts that get deleted regularly do not need paid support

If your organization uses AWS Control Tower to vend accounts, you can set a default support plan in the Account Factory configuration. This ensures new accounts start with the intended plan rather than Basic.

# List support plan for the current account
aws support describe-support-level

# In a multi-account setup, assume a role in each account to check their plans:
aws sts assume-role \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::MEMBER_ACCOUNT_ID:role/OrganizationAccountAccessRole \
  --role-session-name support-check

# Then with those credentials:
aws support describe-support-level

# Note: the support API endpoint is global (us-east-1)
# Always use --region us-east-1 if your CLI defaults elsewhere:
aws support describe-support-level --region us-east-1
Cost Calculation Examples
Support plan pricing uses whichever is greater: the plan minimum or the percentage of monthly AWS charges. Working through a few examples makes the pricing structure concrete:
Example A — startup at $800/month AWS spend:

Developer plan would cost max($29, 3% x $800) = max($29, $24) = $29. Business plan would cost max($100, 10% x $800) = max($100, $80) = $100. Business plan is $71/month more for 24/7 phone support and all Trusted Advisor checks — usually worth it for production.
Example B — growing company at $15,000/month AWS spend:

Business plan: max($100, 10% x $3,333 + 7% x $11,667) = max($100, $333 + $817) = $1,150/month. At this spend level, the TAM relationship of Enterprise On-Ramp ($5,500 minimum) starts to look attractive if the team needs architectural guidance.
Example C — large enterprise at $500,000/month AWS spend:

Business plan: roughly 3-5% of spend = $15,000-$25,000/month. Enterprise plan minimum is $15,000/month. At scale, Enterprise support costs nearly the same percentage as Business — the dedicated TAM, 15-minute response, and IEM are effectively included at no marginal cost.

#!/bin/bash
# Calculate support plan costs for a given monthly AWS spend
calculate_support_costs() {
  local spend="$1"
  echo "Monthly AWS Spend: $spend"

  # Developer: $29 minimum or 3%
  local dev_pct=$(echo "$spend * 0.03" | bc)
  local dev_cost=$(echo "if ($dev_pct > 29) $dev_pct else 29" | bc)
  echo "Developer plan: $dev_cost"

  # Business: tiered percentage
  if [ "$spend" -le 10000 ]; then
    local biz_pct=$(echo "$spend * 0.10" | bc)
  elif [ "$spend" -le 80000 ]; then
    local biz_pct=$(echo "1000 + ($spend - 10000) * 0.07" | bc)
  else
    local biz_pct=$(echo "5900 + ($spend - 80000) * 0.05" | bc)
  fi
  local biz_cost=$(echo "if ($biz_pct > 100) $biz_pct else 100" | bc)
  echo "Business plan: $biz_cost"
  echo "Enterprise On-Ramp minimum: $5500"
  echo "Enterprise minimum: $15000"
}

calculate_support_costs 5000
Support Automation: Using the Health API and Trusted Advisor Programmatically

One of the most valuable Business+ features is programmatic access to AWS Health events and Trusted Advisor results. This enables automated alerting, runbook triggers, and compliance reporting that would otherwise require manual console checks.

# Create an EventBridge rule to alert on AWS Health events affecting your account
aws events put-rule \
  --name aws-health-alerts \
  --event-pattern '{
    "source": ["aws.health"],
    "detail-type": ["AWS Health Event"],
    "detail": {
      "eventTypeCategory": ["issue", "scheduledChange"]
    }
  }' \
  --state ENABLED

# Connect the rule to an SNS topic for email/Slack notifications
aws events put-targets \
  --rule aws-health-alerts \
  --targets Id=1,Arn=arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:ops-alerts

# Get currently open health events
aws health describe-events \
  --filter eventStatusCodes=open \
  --query 'events[].{Service:service, Type:eventTypeCode, Region:region}' \
  --output table

# Describe affected resources for an active event
aws health describe-affected-entities \
  --filter eventArns=arn:aws:health:us-east-1::event/EC2/example/event123 \
  --query 'entities[].{Resource:entityValue, Status:statusCode}'

This Health API integration enables automated operations: when AWS announces scheduled maintenance for EC2 instances, your automation can proactively migrate workloads off affected instances before the maintenance window, rather than waiting for an outage.
Support Plan and Compliance Documentation
For organizations subject to compliance frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, HIPAA), the support plan affects available documentation and audit evidence:
Business and above: access to the SLA documentation in AWS Service Terms — the < 1-hour production-down response time is a contractual commitment you can present to auditors
Business and above: AWS Health API access enables automated logging of service events in your SIEM or audit trail
Enterprise: TAM-led Well-Architected Reviews generate a formal review report that many auditors accept as evidence of security and operational practice reviews
All plans: AWS Artifact (compliance.amazonaws.com) is free — it provides AWS SOC reports, PCI compliance packages, and ISO certifications regardless of support plan
Enterprise: Infrastructure Event Management logs are available as evidence that AWS actively monitored your systems during critical events

The audit value of Enterprise support's TAM relationship is often underappreciated. Having a named AWS contact who can attest to architectural guidance provided, issues escalated, and reviews conducted is qualitatively different from self-service compliance documentation.

# Access AWS Artifact compliance reports (available to all accounts, free)
# Navigate to: console.aws.amazon.com/artifact

# The Artifact API allows downloading compliance reports programmatically
aws artifact get-report-metadata \
  --report-id example-report-id \
  --query 'reportMetadata.{Name:reportName, Type:category}'

# For Business+ plans: pull Health events into your audit log
aws health describe-events \
  --filter '{"eventTypeCategories":["issue"],"startTimes":[{"from":"2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z"}]}' \
  --query 'events[].{Service:service, Event:eventTypeCode, Start:startTime, End:endTime, Status:statusCode}' \
  --output json > health-audit-log.json

Before signing a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for HIPAA compliance, ensure your support plan is Business or above. AWS requires Business+ support for accounts that process PHI under a BAA — Basic and Developer plan accounts are not eligible to sign a BAA.
Alternatives and Supplements to AWS Support Plans
AWS Support plans are not the only source of technical help. Understanding the ecosystem around official support helps you build a complete support strategy:
AWS re:Post (community.aws): the official AWS community forum where engineers and community members answer technical questions — available for free to all accounts; AWS employees monitor and respond
AWS Partner Network (APN): managed service providers (MSPs) with AWS expertise can provide ongoing operational support that often includes AWS support plan costs in their managed service fees
AWS Training and Certification: investing in team certification (Cloud Practitioner, Solutions Architect) reduces support case volume by building internal expertise
AWS Solutions Library: pre-built, tested reference implementations for common patterns — reduces the need for custom development guidance
GitHub aws-samples organization: official AWS code samples for nearly every service — often more current than documentation examples

Many organizations use Business support for the programmatic tools (Health API, Trusted Advisor) and supplement with an APN partner for hands-on operational support. This combination can be more cost-effective than Enterprise support while covering both the automation and human expertise needs.
Summary

AWS Support plans span from the free Basic tier (forums and documentation only) to the $15,000/month Enterprise tier (dedicated TAM, 15-minute critical response). The Business plan at $100/month minimum is the right starting point for any production workload — it unlocks 24/7 phone and chat access, all 500+ Trusted Advisor checks, the Health API for programmatic alerting, and third-party software support.

Developer support fills the gap for non-production accounts that occasionally need email guidance. Enterprise tiers make economic sense for large AWS spends or workloads where the TAM relationship and sub-30-minute critical response justify the cost. Plans can be changed at any time; upgrades take effect immediately, downgrades at the start of the next billing period.
